title: Hormuz Ceasefire Cracks as Tanker Attacks Resume, OPEC Faces Iraqi Exit Threat
subhead: Two consecutive days of tanker strikes in the Strait of Hormuz shattered a week-old ceasefire between the US and Iran, while Iraq warned it may follow the UAE out of OPEC if its production quota isn't raised. France seized a second Russian shadow fleet tanker in the Mediterranean.

sections.heading: Hormuz Ceasefire Unravels as Second Tanker Struck
sections.body:
The interim US-Iran ceasefire, signed roughly one week ago, is under severe strain after two consecutive days of tanker attacks and retaliatory strikes. On Thursday, an Iranian one-way attack drone struck the Singapore-flagged cargo vessel M/V Ever Lovely transiting the Strait of Hormuz. The US responded Friday with strikes on Iranian missile and drone storage sites near the port of Sirik. Then on Saturday, the Panama-flagged tanker M/T Kiku — carrying over two million barrels of crude for Qatar's state-run energy company — was hit by a drone while using an Omani coastal route that vessels have increasingly favored to avoid Iranian-controlled waters.

The US responded with a second, larger round of strikes overnight, hitting 10 Iranian military targets including surveillance infrastructure, communications systems, air defense sites, drone storage facilities, and minelaying capabilities, CENTCOM confirmed. Trump warned on Truth Social that Iran 'will no longer exist' if forced to complete the job militarily.

Iran's Revolutionary Guard retaliated by targeting what it called 'US terrorist army locations in the region.' Bahrain's Foreign Ministry confirmed Iranian drones struck the kingdom, which hosts the US Navy's Fifth Fleet. Kuwait's military said it intercepted incoming Iranian drones and missiles — the first attack on Kuwait since the ceasefire was signed. No US casualties were reported in either country, per US officials cited by multiple outlets.

The IMO halted its efforts to evacuate hundreds of stranded commercial vessels from the Persian Gulf following the Ever Lovely attack, and the UK Maritime Trade Operations agency raised its Hormuz threat assessment to 'substantial.' The Joint Maritime Information Centre also raised its threat level.
sections.whyItMatters: Two tanker strikes in 48 hours, followed by US strikes on 10 Iranian targets and Iranian retaliation reaching Kuwait and Bahrain, mark the worst escalation since the ceasefire. The IMO evacuation halt means hundreds of trapped vessels are stuck again. The Kiku was carrying Qatari crude — Qatar is a key ceasefire negotiator, making this strike particularly destabilizing.

sections.heading: Iran Asserts Control Over Hormuz Shipping Lanes
sections.body:
Iran has moved to formalize control over commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, requiring vessel operators to obtain Iranian permission before transit and threatening ships that do not comply, the New York Times and Reuters reported. Iranian state television said the Revolutionary Guards fired 'warning shots' toward vessels attempting to use channels not approved by Tehran.

In response, the Joint Maritime Information Centre, overseen by the US Navy, announced it would expand the Omani coastal shipping route to accommodate both inbound and outbound traffic — creating a direct challenge to Iran's attempt to control passage. The Kiku was reportedly using this alternative route when it was struck.

Windward analyst Michelle Wiese Bockmann told the NYT that 'shipping is literally caught in the crossfire as the US and Iran battle for control of the Hormuz Strait,' adding that the strikes 'do little to restore confidence that security and safety can be guaranteed.' StealthGas CEO Harry Vafias said one of three vessels stranded in the Gulf for over three months had managed to leave, but 'the other two are still stuck, and it is too difficult for them to exit at this time.'
sections.whyItMatters: A dual-track system is emerging: Iran demands ships use its sanctioned lanes, while the US backs an Omani corridor. The Kiku strike on that very corridor signals Iran will contest any bypass route. This is no longer just about transit safety — it's about who controls access to the world's most important oil chokepoint.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Rebound Risks as Brief Calm Shatters
sections.body:
Brent crude had fallen to around $72/barrel mid-week — its lowest since before the Iran war began — as the ceasefire and resumed Hormuz traffic briefly eased supply fears. Reuters reported that Brent plunged 20% across June, with more tankers exiting the strait after the deal was signed. India's Financial Express noted that Brent below $75 could ease FY27 inflation and current account deficit risks if prices held.

But the weekend's escalation has thrown that optimism into doubt. The IMO's evacuation halt and UKMTO's 'substantial' threat level upgrade signal that shipping insurance costs and delays will spike again. Pakistan's Petroleum Minister Ali Pervaiz Malik said the government will review international oil price indicators even as global prices returned close to pre-war levels — reflecting how import-dependent economies remain vulnerable to any reversal.

The Economist had earlier reported that oil prices were likely to stay elevated for months even with the Iran deal. This weekend's events bear that out: any sustained Hormuz disruption will quickly push Brent back above $80.
sections.whyItMatters: The brief mid-week price dip was a false dawn. Two tanker attacks and the IMO evacuation halt mean the Hormuz premium is returning. Import-dependent economies in South and Southeast Asia that had started adjusting baselines downward will face renewed pressure.

sections.heading: Iraq Threatens OPEC Exit Over Production Quota
sections.body:
Iraq's Oil Ministry warned Thursday that it will consider leaving OPEC if its demand for a higher production quota is not met, according to The National and Reuters. Spokesman Salim Al Rikabi said Iraq 'remains committed to working within the organisation's mechanisms' but added: 'If that does not happen, there will be a decision regarding staying in or exiting the organisation.'

Iraq, OPEC's second-largest producer, pumped just 1.48 million bpd in May — down from nearly 4.2 million bpd in February before the Hormuz closure cut off its export routes. Its July quota stands at 4.378 million bpd, but current output is far below that because of the disruption. Iraq depends on oil for at least 90% of government revenue, and the Hormuz crisis has devastated its finances.

Later Thursday, the ministry issued a clarification stating that reports of Baghdad planning to leave 'do not reflect the government's official position.' It noted that OPEC+ has launched a process to reassess maximum sustainable production capacity and is gradually unwinding voluntary cuts, which would raise Iraq's effective ceiling.

This comes less than two months after the UAE left OPEC on May 1, citing a mismatch between its rising production capacity (4.85 million bpd) and its quota. Iraq is one of OPEC's five founding members — the organisation was formed in Baghdad in 1960.
sections.whyItMatters: Iraq's threat is partly a bargaining posture, but the underlying math is brutal: the Hormuz closure has slashed its oil income by two-thirds while its OPEC quota remains unchanged. If Iraq follows the UAE out, OPEC loses its co-founder and second-largest producer — a potentially fatal blow to the cartel's credibility and market management ability.

sections.heading: France Seizes Second Russian Shadow Fleet Tanker
sections.body:
France's navy seized another oil tanker linked to Russia's shadow fleet on Thursday, Reuters reported. President Macron announced the interception, calling it part of escalating European enforcement of sanctions on Moscow's oil revenue streams. The vessel was taken to waters near Marseille on Friday after being seized near Sicily.

This follows the UK's seizure of a shadow fleet tanker in the English Channel and Canada's imposition of new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet and energy revenues at the G7 summit. The coordinated European action represents a significant escalation in sanctions enforcement — moving from financial sanctions to physical interdiction of vessels.

The shadow fleet — estimated at 400-600 vessels — has been Russia's primary mechanism for moving crude above the $60-per-barrel price cap imposed by the G7. France's second seizure in quick succession signals that European navies are now actively disrupting this network rather than just tracking it.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe has shifted from monitoring Russia's shadow fleet to physically seizing its ships. If France and the UK sustain this tempo, Moscow loses not just the cargo but the vessel itself — a much heavier penalty than insurance denial or port bans. Russia's ability to sustain above-cap crude exports depends on these ships.

sections.heading: Ukraine Strikes Russian Refinery in Krasnodar Krai
sections.body:
Ukrainian drones struck an oil refinery in Slavyansk-na-Kubani in Russia's Krasnodar Krai, with footage showing significant fires at the facility, multiple outlets reported. Ukraine's military has been conducting deep drone strikes reaching up to 1,200 miles into Russian territory, targeting oil refineries and depots, according to NPR.

Ukraine also struck a major industrial facility in Volgograd, President Zelenskyy confirmed Saturday. Russia's Defense Ministry reported a drone attack killed one person in Ukraine on the same day.

The refinery strikes are part of Ukraine's sustained campaign to degrade Russia's domestic fuel production capacity. Krasnodar Krai, in southern Russia, houses several major refineries that supply both the domestic market and military logistics for the southern front.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's long-range drone campaign is eroding Russia's refining capacity in the south, forcing Moscow to either divert fuel from other regions or import refined products — a reversal for a major oil producer. Each strike adds to cumulative damage that Russia's repair crews struggle to keep pace with.

sections.heading: US Sanctions Target Rwanda-Linked Mineral Networks in Congo
sections.body:
The United States imposed new sanctions targeting Rwanda-linked mineral networks accused of financing armed groups in eastern Congo, according to MSN. The measures target smuggling operations that funnel tin, tungsten, and tantalum — conflict minerals critical to electronics supply chains — through Rwandan export channels.

The sanctions come as Rwanda's involvement in eastern Congo has drawn increasing international scrutiny. The mineral networks fund M23 and other armed groups that control mining areas in North Kivu, creating a direct link between conflict financing and global tech supply chains.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first major US action targeting the mineral-smuggling pipeline that sustains conflict in eastern Congo. If enforced effectively, it could disrupt tin and tantalum flows through Rwandan transit — though past sanctions on conflict minerals have been circumvented through rerouting and shell companies.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire is failing in real time: two tankers struck in 48 hours, US strikes on 10 Iranian targets, and Iranian retaliation reaching Kuwait and Bahrain for the first time since the deal. The IMO has halted vessel evacuations from the Gulf, and the Hormuz threat level is back at 'substantial.' Separately, Iraq's threat to leave OPEC — coming just weeks after the UAE's exit — signals the cartel faces an existential credibility crisis driven by the very Hormuz disruption that Iraq's threat references. European navies are now physically seizing Russian shadow fleet tankers rather than just sanctioning them, and Ukraine's deep drone strikes continue to degrade Russian refining capacity. The global energy system is under simultaneous pressure at three chokepoints: Hormuz, the shadow fleet routes, and Russian domestic refineries.
sources: Reuters, Swarajya Mag, New Indian Express, News18, The National, Anadolu Agency, MSNBC/MSN, NPR, MSN, The Hindu, Moneycontrol, Financial Express, Windward/TNT, CENTCOM
